_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against DSM-5-TR criteria, ICD-10 F31, 2018 CANMAT/ISBD bipolar guideline (Yatham), 2020 APA practice guideline, Cipriani 2013 BMJ lithium and suicide meta-analysis, BALANCE trial, STEP-BD, Calabrese 2005 BOLDER quetiapine in bipolar depression, Loebel 2014 lurasidone monotherapy, Hirschfeld 2000 MDQ validation, and 988/WHO suicide-safety language.

h1Title: Bipolar Disorder: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Bipolar disorder is a recurrent mood illness defined by discrete episodes of mania or hypomania alternating with episodes of major depression, separated by intervals of relative wellness. About 2.8% of US adults meet criteria in a given year and roughly 4.4% over a lifetime, with a global prevalence near 1% for bipolar I and another 1-2% for bipolar II. The first episode usually appears in the late teens or early twenties; depression is the more common presenting pole, which is why average time from first symptom to correct diagnosis still runs 6-10 years. Untreated bipolar disorder carries a lifetime suicide risk of 10-15%, but lithium, quetiapine, lurasidone, valproate, lamotrigine, and structured psychotherapy reduce relapses by half or more. If you are thinking of suicide, call or text 988 in the United States.

keyStats.demographics: Bipolar I roughly equal in men and women; bipolar II more common in women; men present earlier and with more mania, women with more depression and rapid cycling
keyStats.avgAge: Median age of first onset 20-25; ~75% of cases begin before age 30
keyStats.globalCases: ~40 million people worldwide live with bipolar disorder (WHO Global Burden of Disease 2019)

definition: Bipolar disorder (ICD-10: F31; DSM-5-TR 296.xx) is a chronic episodic mood disorder characterized by at least one lifetime episode of mania (bipolar I) or hypomania plus a major depressive episode (bipolar II). A manic episode requires a distinct period of abnormally and persistently elevated, expansive, or irritable mood with increased energy lasting at least 7 days or any duration requiring hospitalization, plus three or more of seven DSM-5-TR symptoms: inflated self-esteem or grandiosity, decreased need for sleep, pressured speech, flight of ideas, distractibility, increased goal-directed activity or psychomotor agitation, and excessive involvement in high-risk pleasurable activities. Hypomania requires the same symptom cluster for at least 4 consecutive days but without psychotic features and without marked functional impairment. Bipolar depressive episodes meet the same criteria as unipolar major depression but more often feature hypersomnia, hyperphagia, leaden paralysis, and psychomotor retardation. The bipolar spectrum also includes cyclothymic disorder (chronic subthreshold mood fluctuation for at least 2 years), bipolar disorder with mixed features, and rapid cycling (four or more episodes in 12 months). Pathophysiology involves dysregulated dopaminergic and glutamatergic signaling, mitochondrial dysfunction, circadian instability, and a strong polygenic risk burden overlapping with schizophrenia. Bipolar disorder is managed by psychiatry; primary care provides screening, continuity care, and metabolic monitoring of mood stabilizers and antipsychotics.

typesClassification.description: At least one lifetime manic episode lasting 7+ days or requiring hospitalization. Major depressive episodes are common but not required for diagnosis. Lifetime psychosis risk in mania is ~50%; chronicity and functional impairment are typically greater than in bipolar II.

At least one hypomanic episode (4+ days, no psychosis, no severe impairment) plus at least one major depressive episode. Depression dominates the long-term course and accounts for most of the disability and suicide risk; misdiagnosis as unipolar depression is common.
Two or more years of fluctuating subthreshold hypomanic and depressive symptoms that never meet full episode criteria. About 15-50% progress to bipolar I or II over time.
Manic or hypomanic episode that simultaneously meets at least three depressive criteria, or vice versa. Mixed states carry the highest suicide risk and respond poorly to antidepressant monotherapy.

Four or more mood episodes in 12 months that meet full criteria and are separated by partial or full remission or by a switch of polarity. More common in women, in bipolar II, and in patients exposed to antidepressant monotherapy or untreated hypothyroidism.

typesClassification.type: Bipolar disorder, other specified or unspecified (F31.89, F31.9)
typesClassification.description: Episodes with bipolar features that fall short of full duration or symptom thresholds. Still warrants close monitoring because conversion to bipolar I or II is common.

primarySymptoms: Distinct manic episodes of elevated, expansive, or irritable mood with increased energy lasting at least 7 days, often beginning abruptly and noticed first by family rather than the patient.
Decreased need for sleep during mania — feeling rested after only 2-3 hours, sometimes going days without sleep while remaining energetic and goal-driven.
Pressured rapid speech that is difficult to interrupt, flight of ideas with racing thoughts, and easy distractibility by irrelevant stimuli.
Inflated self-esteem or grandiosity, which can reach delusional intensity (special powers, special mission, special identity) in severe mania with psychotic features.
Excessive involvement in high-risk pleasurable activities during manic or hypomanic episodes — uncontrolled spending, sexual indiscretions, unwise investments, reckless driving, substance binges.

Hypomanic episodes lasting 4 or more days that look like productive bursts of energy and creativity but are still abnormal for the patient and noticed by others; the patient often denies illness during hypomania.

Major depressive episodes with hypersomnia, hyperphagia, leaden paralysis, anhedonia, slowed thinking, and intense fatigue — bipolar depression typically tilts toward retarded rather than agitated features.
Recurrent thoughts of death and suicidal ideation, particularly during depressive episodes and mixed states; lifetime suicide attempt rate is 30-40% in bipolar disorder.
Psychotic features in severe mania (50% lifetime in bipolar I) or severe depression — usually mood-congruent grandiose, paranoid, or nihilistic delusions and occasionally hallucinations.

Mixed episodes that combine manic energy, racing thoughts, and irritability with depressive hopelessness and suicidal ideation — these states feel particularly intolerable and carry the highest acute risk.
Intervals of relative euthymia between episodes during which mood and function approach baseline; many patients still have residual subsyndromal symptoms, cognitive slowing, and sleep instability.
earlyWarningSigns: Sustained reduction in sleep need without daytime fatigue — often the single most reliable early sign of an emerging manic or hypomanic episode
Acceleration of speech, thought, or activity noticed by family or coworkers before the patient recognizes it
New plans, projects, or financial decisions that feel unusually urgent and important
Increased irritability, argumentativeness, or impulsivity in a previously even-tempered person
Return of early morning awakening, hopelessness, or loss of interest after a period of stability — suggests a depressive episode is starting
emergencySigns: Active suicidal ideation with a plan, intent, or access to means — call 988 (US Suicide & Crisis Lifeline), go to an emergency department, or call local emergency services
Psychotic mania with grandiose or paranoid delusions, severe agitation, or threats of violence — urgent psychiatric evaluation and likely inpatient admission
Severe sleep deprivation (≥48 hours without sleep) during a manic episode — risk of delirium, exhaustion collapse, and rapid escalation
Mixed episode with intense agitation plus suicidal ideation — the highest acute suicide-risk presentation in bipolar disorder
Catatonia — immobility, mutism, posturing, or extreme negativism — psychiatric emergency requiring benzodiazepine challenge or ECT
Postpartum mania or psychosis — onset within 2 weeks of childbirth with rapid mood shifts, paranoia, or thoughts of harm to self or infant — immediate psychiatric admission

causes.description: Heritability is the highest of any major psychiatric illness — 60-85% in twin studies. First-degree relatives have a 5-10× higher lifetime risk. Genome-wide studies have identified over 60 risk loci with substantial overlap with schizophrenia and major depression.

Bipolar disorder involves dysregulated dopamine and glutamate signaling, abnormal anterior cingulate-amygdala-prefrontal connectivity, mitochondrial dysfunction, and altered intracellular signaling (GSK-3β, inositol monophosphatase) that are the targets of lithium and valproate.

Disrupted sleep-wake cycles can both trigger and reflect episodes. Sleep deprivation reliably induces hypomania or mania in vulnerable individuals; circadian-stabilizing therapies (interpersonal and social rhythm therapy) reduce relapse independent of medication.

Childhood adversity is reported in roughly 50% of patients and predicts earlier onset, more rapid cycling, and worse treatment response. Acute psychosocial stressors precipitate the first episode in over half of cases, and stress kindles vulnerability to subsequent episodes.

Cannabis, stimulants, hallucinogens, and alcohol can precipitate first manic episodes and worsen the long-term course. Substance use disorders coexist with bipolar disorder in roughly 40-60% of patients and double suicide risk.

Antidepressant monotherapy can precipitate mania or rapid cycling in 10-25% of patients with undiagnosed bipolar disorder. Corticosteroids, dopamine agonists, isoniazid, and stimulants are also documented triggers.

The postpartum period carries a 23-fold increase in risk of a manic or psychotic episode in women with bipolar disorder. Perimenopause and the late luteal phase of the menstrual cycle also destabilize mood for many patients.

riskFactors.description: A first-degree relative with bipolar disorder raises lifetime risk 5-10×; concordance reaches 40-70% in monozygotic twins. Family history of schizophrenia or recurrent major depression also raises risk modestly.

riskFactors.factor: Younger age of first depressive episode
riskFactors.description: Depression onset before age 25, especially with atypical features (hypersomnia, hyperphagia, leaden paralysis) or psychosis, raises the probability that underlying illness is bipolar by 2-4×.

riskFactors.factor: Postpartum status in women with mood-disorder history
riskFactors.description: The first 2-4 weeks after delivery carry a 23-fold elevated risk of bipolar episodes, including postpartum psychosis. Prophylactic mood stabilization in pregnancy is recommended for those with prior episodes.
Reported by 40-60% of patients and associated with earlier onset, more rapid cycling, more suicide attempts, and worse functional outcome. Trauma-focused psychotherapy improves comorbid PTSD.

Cannabis, stimulants, alcohol, and hallucinogens precipitate first episodes and worsen course. Comorbid substance use disorders occur in 40-60% of bipolar patients and double suicide risk; integrated treatment is essential.

Antidepressant monotherapy in patients with unrecognized bipolar disorder triggers mania or hypomania in 10-25%. Screening every depressed patient with the MDQ before starting an antidepressant is recommended.
Shift work, jet lag, and acute sleep loss reliably induce mood episodes. Stable sleep-wake schedules reduce relapse risk in interpersonal and social rhythm therapy trials.
Present in roughly 50% of bipolar patients and associated with more rapid cycling, more substance use, more suicide attempts, and slower treatment response.
Subclinical hypothyroidism is a risk factor for rapid cycling and treatment resistance. Annual TSH monitoring is standard, especially for patients on lithium.
Paternal age over 45 is associated with a modest 1.3-1.4× increase in offspring bipolar risk, mediated by de novo mutations in spermatogenesis.

affectedDemographics: US prevalence: roughly 2.8% of adults annually and 4.4% lifetime (NCS-R); rates are similar across racial and ethnic groups when assessed with structured interviews
Onset peaks between ages 15 and 25; 75% of cases begin before age 30, but late-onset (>50) cases occur and warrant medical workup
Bipolar I is roughly equally common in men and women; bipolar II is roughly twice as common in women
Cultural and clinical factors lead to under-diagnosis in Black patients and over-diagnosis of schizophrenia in the same group; structured assessment narrows this gap
Bipolar disorder accounts for roughly 7 million disability-adjusted life years globally each year (WHO Global Burden of Disease 2019)
diagnosisOverview: Bipolar disorder is a clinical diagnosis built on a careful longitudinal mood history, collateral information from family or partners, and validated screening tools. The single most important diagnostic question is whether the patient has ever had a manic or hypomanic episode, because the average gap between first depressive episode and correct bipolar diagnosis is still 6-10 years. The Mood Disorder Questionnaire (MDQ, Hirschfeld 2000) is a 13-item self-report that screens for past hypomania or mania with 73% sensitivity and 90% specificity for bipolar I; the Hypomania Checklist (HCL-32) is more sensitive for bipolar II. The Composite International Diagnostic Interview and the Structured Clinical Interview for DSM-5 (SCID-5) are gold-standard structured interviews used in research and complex cases. DSM-5-TR requires that mania last at least 7 days (or any duration if hospitalization is required), and hypomania at least 4 days, with the symptom cluster representing a distinct change from baseline that is observable to others. Every patient presenting with depression should be specifically asked about past hypomania, family psychiatric history, postpartum episodes, antidepressant-induced switches, and substance-induced mood changes — these features substantially raise the prior probability of bipolarity. Medical mimics must be excluded: TSH for hyperthyroidism, complete metabolic panel, vitamin B12, urine toxicology, and HIV testing where appropriate. Brain imaging is not routinely required but is indicated for atypical presentations, late-onset cases, or neurological signs. Suicide-risk assessment using the Columbia Scale (C-SSRS) is mandatory at every visit. Once diagnosed, severity is rated with the Young Mania Rating Scale (YMRS) for mania and the Montgomery-Åsberg Depression Rating Scale (MADRS) or PHQ-9 for depression to track response to treatment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: 13-item self-report screen for past or current hypomania or mania. Validated by Hirschfeld 2000 with 73% sensitivity and 90% specificity for bipolar I disorder. Recommended by APA before starting any antidepressant.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A brief self-report form completed in 3-5 minutes in the waiting room or via patient portal. A positive screen prompts a structured clinical interview.

diagnosticTests.purpose: 32-item self-report with higher sensitivity than the MDQ for bipolar II and the softer hypomanic spectrum. Useful when the MDQ is negative but bipolarity is still suspected.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Self-report taking 5-10 minutes; positive screen prompts longitudinal mood history and family interview.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard interview for confirming DSM-5-TR diagnosis. Distinguishes bipolar I, bipolar II, cyclothymia, substance-induced mood disorder, and unipolar depression with high reliability.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 45-90 minute structured interview by a trained psychiatrist or psychologist; often supplemented by collateral history from a family member or partner.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Eleven-item clinician-rated severity scale for mania, used to track treatment response and define remission (YMRS <8) in trials and clinical care.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Brief structured assessment by the clinician taking 10-15 minutes during a visit; repeated at intervals to follow trajectory.

diagnosticTests.test: MADRS or PHQ-9 for depressive episodes
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies depression severity and tracks response. PHQ-9 ≥10 is the standard threshold for clinically significant depression; MADRS is preferred in trials.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Brief self-report (PHQ-9) or clinician interview (MADRS), 5-15 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Standardized suicide-risk screen used at every bipolar visit. Stratifies passive ideation, active ideation, plan, intent, and behaviors to guide safety planning and disposition.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Six initial yes/no questions taking 1-2 minutes; expands with follow-up questions if positive.

diagnosticTests.test: TSH, comprehensive metabolic panel, CBC, vitamin B12, vitamin D, urine toxicology
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes hyperthyroidism, hypothyroidism, electrolyte abnormalities, anemia, vitamin deficiencies, and substance-induced mood states that mimic bipolar episodes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard fasting blood draw plus urine sample; results in 1-2 days. Baseline labs are also required before starting lithium, valproate, or atypical antipsychotics.

diagnosticTests.test: Renal panel, ECG, and pregnancy test before lithium
diagnosticTests.purpose: Lithium requires baseline creatinine and eGFR, electrolytes, calcium, and ECG (especially after age 40 or with cardiac history). A pregnancy test and contraception plan are required in women of childbearing potential before lithium or valproate.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw, 12-lead ECG, and pregnancy test; same-day results in most outpatient psychiatry clinics.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of bipolar disorder splits across three phases: acute mania or hypomania, acute bipolar depression, and long-term maintenance. For acute mania, first-line monotherapy options recommended by the 2018 CANMAT/ISBD guideline and the 2020 APA practice guideline include lithium, valproate (divalproex), quetiapine, aripiprazole, asenapine, cariprazine, paliperidone, risperidone, and olanzapine. Severe mania, mixed features, or psychotic mania typically require combination therapy — lithium or valproate plus an atypical antipsychotic — and often inpatient admission. For acute bipolar depression, the strongest evidence supports quetiapine (Calabrese 2005 BOLDER), lurasidone monotherapy or adjunctive (Loebel 2014), cariprazine, olanzapine-fluoxetine combination, and lamotrigine for relapse prevention; antidepressant monotherapy is avoided because of switch and cycling risk. For maintenance, lithium remains the benchmark with the largest evidence base, the broadest efficacy across mania and depression, and the unique mortality benefit of reducing suicide risk by roughly 60% in long-term users (Cipriani 2013 BMJ meta-analysis). The BALANCE trial established that lithium monotherapy outperforms valproate monotherapy for relapse prevention, and that lithium plus valproate is superior to valproate alone. Quetiapine, lamotrigine (especially for depressive recurrence), aripiprazole long-acting injectable, and combinations are also evidence-based maintenance options. Adjunctive psychotherapies — cognitive behavioral therapy adapted for bipolar disorder, family-focused therapy, interpersonal and social rhythm therapy, and group psychoeducation — reduce relapse rates by an additional 25-40% over medication alone (STEP-BD). ECT is the most effective acute treatment for severe, psychotic, catatonic, or refractory episodes with response rates of 60-80%, and remains underused. Lifestyle pillars are sleep regularity, abstinence from cannabis and stimulants, treatment of comorbid anxiety, and metabolic monitoring of weight, lipids, glucose, thyroid, and renal function. The treatment plan is lifelong for most patients; stopping mood stabilizers, especially lithium, abruptly is associated with relapse and rebound suicide risk and should be avoided.

medicalTreatments.name: Lithium carbonate (target serum level 0.6-0.8 mmol/L maintenance; 0.8-1.2 mmol/L acute mania)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line mood stabilizer with the largest evidence base across acute mania, maintenance, and suicide prevention. Inhibits GSK-3β and inositol monophosphatase; the only agent shown to reduce suicide mortality. Requires baseline and periodic monitoring of renal function, thyroid, calcium, and serum level.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Acute mania response in 60-70% by week 3-4. Maintenance reduces overall relapse by ~40% and reduces suicide death by approximately 60% versus placebo or alternative mood stabilizers (Cipriani 2013 BMJ meta-analysis).

medicalTreatments.name: Valproate (divalproex sodium, target serum level 50-125 µg/mL)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for acute mania, particularly with mixed features or rapid cycling. Faster titration than lithium. Teratogenic — contraindicated in pregnancy and in women of childbearing potential without effective contraception due to neural tube defects and reduced offspring IQ.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Acute mania response in 50-60% by 3 weeks; comparable to lithium for acute mania but inferior to lithium for long-term relapse prevention in the BALANCE trial.

medicalTreatments.name: Quetiapine XR (300-800 mg at bedtime)
medicalTreatments.description: Atypical antipsychotic with FDA approval for acute mania, acute bipolar depression, and maintenance. One of the few agents with strong evidence for the depressive pole. Main limitations are sedation, weight gain, and metabolic syndrome; baseline and periodic metabolic monitoring is required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Acute bipolar depression response 58% vs 36% placebo at 8 weeks (Calabrese 2005 BOLDER); manic response 55-60% by week 3; maintenance reduces relapse by ~50%.

medicalTreatments.name: Lurasidone (20-120 mg daily with food ≥350 kcal)
medicalTreatments.description: Atypical antipsychotic FDA-approved for bipolar I depression as monotherapy and adjunct to lithium or valproate. Lower metabolic and weight burden than quetiapine or olanzapine. Common side effects are akathisia and nausea.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Bipolar I depression response rate 53% with monotherapy vs 30% placebo at 6 weeks (Loebel 2014); similar adjunctive efficacy added to lithium or valproate.

medicalTreatments.name: Lamotrigine (titrate to 200 mg daily over 6-8 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Anticonvulsant mood stabilizer with strong evidence for preventing depressive relapse in bipolar I and II. Requires slow titration to minimize risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome and toxic epidermal necrolysis (incidence ~0.1% with proper titration). Not first-line for acute mania.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Maintenance reduces depressive relapse by ~40-50% versus placebo; smaller effect on manic relapse. Strongest evidence in patients with depression-predominant course.

medicalTreatments.name: Aripiprazole (10-30 mg daily) or its long-acting injectable (400 mg monthly)
medicalTreatments.description: Partial dopamine D2/D3 agonist with FDA approval for acute mania, mixed episodes, and maintenance. Lower weight and metabolic burden than olanzapine or quetiapine. The long-acting injectable improves adherence in patients with frequent relapses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Acute mania response in 50-60% by week 3; LAI reduced relapse by 45% versus placebo over 1 year (Calabrese 2017).

medicalTreatments.name: Cariprazine (1.5-6 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: D3-preferring partial agonist approved for both acute mania and acute bipolar I depression. Useful when patients tolerate aripiprazole-class agents but need broader efficacy across mood poles.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Bipolar depression response 53% versus 33% placebo (Earley 2019); acute mania response 60% by week 3.

medicalTreatments.name: Olanzapine-fluoxetine combination (Symbyax 6/25 to 12/50 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: FDA-approved for acute bipolar I depression. Effective but limited by olanzapine's weight gain and metabolic effects; usually reserved for patients who fail quetiapine or lurasidone.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rate ~56% versus 30% placebo at 8 weeks (Tohen 2003); among the strongest effect sizes for acute bipolar depression.

medicalTreatments.name: Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT), 6-12 sessions
medicalTreatments.description: Most effective acute treatment for severe, psychotic, catatonic, or treatment-resistant mania, mixed episodes, and bipolar depression. Bitemporal placement is typically preferred for mania; right-unilateral for depression. Safe in pregnancy and the elderly.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Acute response rates 60-80% — the highest of any bipolar treatment in severe or refractory illness.

medicalTreatments.name: Clozapine (titrate carefully to 200-600 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for treatment-resistant mania, mixed states, or schizoaffective bipolar type. Requires weekly absolute neutrophil count monitoring for the first 6 months due to 0.4-1% agranulocytosis risk. Uniquely effective in refractory cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response in 60-70% of patients who have failed multiple alternatives; reduces suicide-related events similar to lithium in mood-disorder subgroups.

surgicalOptions.name: Deep brain stimulation (DBS) — investigational
surgicalOptions.description: Targeted stimulation of subcallosal cingulate or nucleus accumbens has been explored for severe, treatment-refractory bipolar depression at academic centers. Not FDA-approved for bipolar disorder; reserved for research protocols.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Small open-label series report response in 30-50%; randomized data are limited and the procedure is not standard of care.

surgicalOptions.name: Vagus nerve stimulation (VNS)
surgicalOptions.description: FDA-approved for chronic, treatment-resistant unipolar depression and has been used adjunctively in some treatment-resistant bipolar depression cases. An implanted device delivers intermittent left cervical vagus stimulation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Response rates in chronic refractory mood disorders ~30-40% by 1-2 years; benefit is delayed compared to most pharmacologic options.

alternativeTreatments.name: Bright-light therapy (10,000 lux, 30-60 minutes, midday in bipolar depression)
alternativeTreatments.description: Sit-EFFECT and Sit 2018 randomized trials showed remission rates roughly twice placebo when added to a mood stabilizer for bipolar I depression. Morning timing carries higher switch risk in bipolar disorder; midday dosing is preferred.

alternativeTreatments.name: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA-predominant, ≥1 g EPA daily)
alternativeTreatments.description: Meta-analyses (Sarris 2012) show a small positive effect on bipolar depression as adjunctive therapy. No effect on mania. Reasonable adjunct; not a substitute for mood stabilizers.

alternativeTreatments.name: Interpersonal and social rhythm therapy (IPSRT)
alternativeTreatments.description: Manualized therapy targeting sleep, daily routine, and interpersonal stressors; the STEP-BD substudy showed reduced relapse and faster recovery from depressive episodes when added to medication.

whySeeSpecialist: Bipolar disorder should be managed by a psychiatrist. Referral is essential for any suspected manic or hypomanic episode, any depressed patient who screens positive on the MDQ, postpartum patients with mood-disorder history, treatment-resistant depression, recurrent depression with atypical features, antidepressant-induced switching, suicidal ideation with bipolar features, and active substance use complicating mood. Primary care has an important continuity role for metabolic monitoring, lithium and valproate levels, and physical-health comorbidities, but treatment selection and adjustment is psychiatry-led.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for board certification by the American Board of Psychiatry and Neurology (ABPN), explicit experience with bipolar disorder rather than only depression, comfort with lithium dosing and monitoring, and familiarity with antipsychotic metabolic monitoring. Integrated behavioral-health programs with psychiatry plus therapy plus care management improve adherence and outcomes. For severe or refractory illness, academic-medical-center programs with ECT, ketamine, and clinical-trial access offer the full spectrum. Continuity over years matters more than prestige; the patient-clinician alliance is the strongest predictor of long-term outcome.
hospitalCriteria: Inpatient psychiatric unit with safety protocols for acute mania, mixed states, suicidality, and psychosis
ECT service with anesthesiology coverage for severe, psychotic, catatonic, or refractory bipolar episodes
Mother-and-baby psychiatric unit or perinatal psychiatry service for postpartum mania and psychosis
Integrated behavioral health with psychiatry, therapy, and care-management roles for outpatient maintenance
Lithium clinic or psychopharmacology service with reliable monitoring of serum levels, renal function, and thyroid
Substance-use treatment program able to coordinate with the bipolar care team
preventionStrategies: Maintain a consistent sleep schedule with the same wake time on weekdays and weekends — irregular sleep is one of the strongest preventable triggers of mood episodes
Avoid cannabis, stimulants, and hallucinogens; alcohol use should be minimal during stable periods and absent during active episodes
Screen every patient with depression — and especially postpartum patients with mood-disorder history — with the MDQ before starting an antidepressant
Identify a relapse-prevention plan with the patient, family, and clinician early, including the patient's earliest warning signs and what to do when they appear
Monitor TSH at least annually, especially for patients on lithium, because subclinical hypothyroidism contributes to rapid cycling
Plan pregnancy and postpartum care in advance with psychiatry and obstetrics; the postpartum period carries 23-fold elevated risk of relapse in untreated patients
lifestyleModifications: Establish a stable sleep-wake schedule, with consistent bedtime and wake time; aim for 7-9 hours of sleep nightly and minimize all-nighters or shift work
Limit alcohol to none during acute episodes and to occasional moderate use during stable periods; many clinicians advise long-term abstinence
Avoid cannabis, stimulants, and recreational hallucinogens entirely — all are documented triggers of manic episodes
Build a structured daily routine with regular meals, exercise, social contact, and sunlight exposure (interpersonal and social rhythm therapy principle)
Engage in 30-45 minutes of moderate aerobic exercise 3-5 days per week — supports mood, sleep, and metabolic health on antipsychotics
Keep a daily mood and sleep log during the first year of treatment; patterns identify personal triggers and early warning signs
Maintain a written safety plan with crisis contacts (988 in the US) and store firearms outside the home during acute episodes
Educate one or two trusted family members about the illness so they can recognize early warning signs and help with treatment decisions when needed

recommended: Mediterranean-style eating pattern — vegetables, fruits, whole grains, legumes, fish, olive oil; associated with lower mood-disorder incidence and helpful for cardiometabolic risk on mood stabilizers
Adequate hydration, especially for patients on lithium — dehydration raises lithium levels and toxicity risk
Steady carbohydrate intake and balanced meals to support metabolic stability on quetiapine, olanzapine, or other atypicals
Omega-3-rich fish 2-3 times weekly, or EPA-predominant supplements as adjunct
Regular protein intake (1.0-1.2 g/kg/day) to support neurotransmitter synthesis and lean mass
avoid: Excess alcohol — destabilizes mood, sleep, and lithium levels; ideally absent during acute episodes
Cannabis edibles and concentrates — among the strongest dietary precipitants of manic episodes
High caffeine intake late in the day, which fragments sleep and can trigger hypomania
Crash diets or prolonged fasting — destabilize blood sugar and disrupt circadian rhythms
Grapefruit juice and St. John's wort with most psychotropic medications — significant CYP3A4 interactions
Sudden major changes in sodium or fluid intake while on lithium — both alter lithium levels and toxicity risk

exerciseGuidelines: Aim for 30-45 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic exercise 3-5 days per week, plus resistance training twice weekly. Exercise supports mood, sleep, and cardiometabolic health, which is particularly important on antipsychotics. During acute mania, exercise should be limited and supervised because patients may push to dangerous extremes; during severe depression, even short walks help. Group-based or routine-anchored exercise reinforces social-rhythm stability.
dailyManagement: Take medication at the same time each day; missing lithium doses materially raises relapse risk and stopping abruptly is associated with rebound mania
Track mood, sleep, and energy in a daily log or app for the first year of treatment and during any dose changes
Recheck lithium level, TSH, and renal function every 6-12 months in stable patients; more often during titration
Maintain consistent wake time, even on weekends and during travel — sleep deprivation is one of the most reliable manic triggers
Keep an updated written crisis plan with early warning signs, coping strategies, prescriber and emergency contacts (988 in the US), and consent for family involvement during severe episodes
Stay hydrated and avoid sudden major changes in salt intake or NSAID use while on lithium
Attend every therapy session and family-focused or psychoeducation group — engagement with psychosocial treatment reduces relapse by 25-40% beyond medication alone
prognosis: With consistent maintenance treatment, most patients experience longer well intervals, fewer hospitalizations, and improved functional outcomes. Lithium reduces overall relapse by roughly 40% and suicide death by approximately 60% in long-term users (Cipriani 2013 BMJ meta-analysis). STEP-BD showed that 58% of patients reached recovery within 2 years, with adjunctive psychotherapy adding 25-40% relapse reduction over medication alone. Despite treatment advances, bipolar disorder remains a chronic relapsing illness — average lifetime episode count is 8-10 untreated, with shortening cycles. Lifetime suicide risk is 10-15% and lifetime attempt rate is 30-40%, the highest of any psychiatric illness. Predictors of better outcome include lithium response, early treatment initiation, stable sleep, social support, absence of substance use, and engagement with psychosocial treatment. Predictors of worse outcome include rapid cycling, mixed features, comorbid substance use, childhood adversity, and antidepressant-induced switching. Functional recovery often lags symptomatic recovery; cognitive remediation, supported employment, and family-focused therapy substantially improve real-world outcomes.
recoveryTimeline: Acute mania typically responds within 1-3 weeks once an effective agent reaches therapeutic level — lithium effects begin at week 1-2 and peak by week 4; antipsychotic effects begin within days. Acute bipolar depression usually responds over 4-8 weeks with quetiapine, lurasidone, or cariprazine. Functional recovery typically lags symptom remission by 3-6 months and may take longer when cognitive symptoms persist. Maintenance treatment is recommended indefinitely after a single severe manic episode and after two or more episodes of any polarity. Lithium discontinuation, even after years of stability, is associated with relapse in roughly 50% within 6 months and a rebound elevation in suicide risk; any discontinuation should be gradual and supervised.
complications: Suicide and suicide attempts — lifetime suicide death rate 10-15%, lifetime attempt rate 30-40%; the highest of any psychiatric illness. Lithium reduces suicide death by ~60% versus alternative regimens
Substance use disorders — alcohol, cannabis, stimulant, and opioid use disorders coexist with bipolar disorder in 40-60% of patients and double suicide risk; integrated treatment is essential

Metabolic syndrome and cardiovascular disease — combined effects of illness, antipsychotic exposure, sedentary lifestyle, and smoking shorten lifespan by 10-20 years; aggressive cardiometabolic monitoring is mandatory
Cognitive impairment, especially in attention, processing speed, and executive function, persisting between episodes in many patients and contributing to functional disability
Occupational and educational disruption — bipolar disorder is among the top global causes of disability-adjusted life years; supported employment improves outcomes
Relationship and family disruption — high rates of divorce, financial harm during manic episodes, and caregiver burden; family-focused therapy and psychoeducation reduce these risks
Lithium-related renal and thyroid effects — chronic kidney disease in 1-5% after decades of use, and hypothyroidism in 10-15%; regular monitoring detects these early

supportResources.name: 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline (US)
supportResources.description: Free 24/7 confidential support for people in suicidal crisis or emotional distress. Call or text 988.

supportResources.name: National Alliance on Mental Illness (NAMI)
supportResources.description: Patient and family education, support groups, advocacy, and a free helpline (1-800-950-NAMI) for people affected by bipolar disorder.

supportResources.name: Depression and Bipolar Support Alliance (DBSA)
supportResources.description: Peer-led support groups, educational resources, and online wellness tools for adults with bipolar disorder and their families.

supportResources.name: International Bipolar Foundation (IBPF)
supportResources.description: Global education and family-support resources, including videos, books, and webinars on bipolar I and II.

confusedWithConditions.name: Depression (Unipolar Major Depressive Disorder)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Unipolar depression lacks lifetime hypomanic or manic episodes. Bipolar disorder requires at least one such episode for diagnosis. Bipolar depression more often features hypersomnia, hyperphagia, leaden paralysis, and earlier onset; antidepressant monotherapy can trigger mania in unrecognized bipolar disorder, so every depressed patient should be screened with the MDQ.

confusedWithConditions.name: Schizophrenia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Schizophrenia features chronic psychosis, prominent negative symptoms, and functional decline that persist outside of mood episodes. Bipolar disorder with psychotic features has psychosis confined to mood episodes with relative recovery between episodes. Misdiagnosis as schizophrenia is common in Black patients and during early psychotic mania.

confusedWithConditions.name: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: ADHD causes lifelong inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity with relatively stable mood. Bipolar disorder produces discrete episodes lasting days to weeks. The two can coexist in 10-20% of cases; chronic, non-episodic mood instability with rapid shifts suggests ADHD or borderline personality disorder rather than bipolar disorder.

confusedWithConditions.name: Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: PTSD follows a discrete traumatic event and is dominated by re-experiencing, avoidance, hyperarousal, and negative cognitions. Bipolar disorder lacks the trauma-specific intrusion symptoms. The two co-occur in roughly 20% of bipolar cases and both contribute to mood instability.

confusedWithConditions.name: Borderline Personality Disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Borderline personality features chronic, rapidly shifting mood states (often within a day), unstable identity, fear of abandonment, and impulsivity that are reactive to interpersonal triggers. Bipolar disorder produces discrete sustained episodes of days to weeks. The two can coexist; dialectical behavior therapy is the evidence-based treatment for borderline features.

relatedConditions.name: Depression
relatedConditions.relevance: Bipolar depression closely resembles unipolar major depression at presentation and is the most common reason for missed bipolar diagnosis.

relatedConditions.name: Schizophrenia
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares roughly 60% of common genetic risk with bipolar disorder; bipolar disorder with psychotic features and schizoaffective disorder sit on the same spectrum.

relatedConditions.name: ADHD
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-occurs in 10-20% of bipolar cases and complicates diagnosis when chronic inattention overlaps with episodic mood symptoms.

relatedConditions.name: PTSD
relatedConditions.relevance: Childhood trauma is common in bipolar disorder; PTSD co-occurs in 15-25% and worsens course and cycling.

relatedConditions.name: Cyclothymic Disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Subthreshold mood fluctuations lasting 2+ years; 15-50% of cases progress to bipolar I or II over time.

faqs.question: What is the difference between bipolar I and bipolar II?
faqs.answer: Bipolar I requires at least one lifetime manic episode lasting 7 or more days, or any duration requiring hospitalization. Bipolar II requires at least one hypomanic episode (4 or more days, no psychosis, no severe impairment) plus at least one major depressive episode. Bipolar II is not a milder illness; depression dominates the long-term course and carries similar suicide risk.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of bipolar disorder?
faqs.answer: Bipolar disorder produces discrete episodes of mania (elevated or irritable mood, decreased need for sleep, racing thoughts, pressured speech, grandiosity, risky behavior) lasting at least 7 days, or hypomania lasting 4 or more days, alternating with major depressive episodes (low mood, anhedonia, fatigue, hypersomnia, hopelessness, suicidal thoughts). Mixed states combine features of both poles simultaneously.

faqs.question: How is bipolar disorder diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is clinical, based on DSM-5-TR criteria gathered through a careful longitudinal mood history and collateral information from family. The Mood Disorder Questionnaire (MDQ) screens for past hypomania or mania with 73% sensitivity and 90% specificity for bipolar I. Structured interviews (SCID-5, MINI) confirm the diagnosis. Basic labs and a urine toxicology screen exclude medical and substance-induced mimics.

faqs.question: Why is bipolar disorder often diagnosed late?
faqs.answer: The first episode is usually depression, and hypomania often feels like productivity rather than illness, so patients rarely report it. The average gap between first depressive episode and correct bipolar diagnosis is still 6-10 years. Routine MDQ screening before starting any antidepressant shortens this delay and reduces the risk of antidepressant-induced mania.

faqs.question: Is bipolar disorder genetic?
faqs.answer: Heritability is 60-85%, the highest of any major psychiatric illness. First-degree relatives have a 5-10× higher lifetime risk. Genes interact with stressors, sleep, substance use, and trauma, so genetic risk alone does not determine who develops bipolar disorder.

faqs.question: What triggers a manic episode?
faqs.answer: Sleep loss is the most consistent trigger; even a single all-nighter can precipitate mania in vulnerable people. Other common triggers include cannabis, stimulants, hallucinogens, alcohol binges, the postpartum period, antidepressant exposure without a mood stabilizer, and major stressors. Identifying personal triggers is a core part of relapse prevention.

faqs.question: How long does a manic episode last?
faqs.answer: Untreated mania typically lasts 3-6 months. With effective treatment (lithium, valproate, or an atypical antipsychotic), most patients see substantial improvement within 1-3 weeks and resolution by 4-8 weeks. Severe, psychotic, or mixed episodes often require inpatient care and may take longer to fully resolve.

faqs.question: What is the best medication for bipolar disorder?
faqs.answer: Lithium is the benchmark mood stabilizer — first-line for acute mania, maintenance, and the only medication shown to reduce suicide deaths. Quetiapine, lurasidone, and cariprazine are first-line for bipolar depression. Valproate and lamotrigine are evidence-based alternatives. Best agent depends on phase (mania vs depression), course (rapid cycling, mixed features), and individual response and tolerability.

faqs.question: Does lithium really prevent suicide?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Cipriani 2013 BMJ meta-analysis of randomized trials showed lithium reduces suicide deaths by roughly 60% compared with placebo or alternative mood stabilizers. This effect appears to be independent of mood-stabilizing action and is unique to lithium. Long-term lithium remains the strongest mortality-reducing treatment in psychiatry.

faqs.question: Can antidepressants cause mania?
faqs.answer: Yes. In patients with unrecognized bipolar disorder, antidepressant monotherapy triggers mania or hypomania in roughly 10-25% of cases. This is why every depressed patient should be screened for bipolar disorder with the MDQ before starting an antidepressant, and why bipolar depression is treated with mood stabilizers or atypical antipsychotics rather than antidepressants alone.

faqs.question: Can people with bipolar disorder live a normal life?
faqs.answer: Yes, many do. With consistent treatment, lifestyle stability, and engagement in therapy, most patients work, study, have relationships, and raise families. Lithium and other mood stabilizers reduce relapse and suicide risk substantially. The illness is chronic, so treatment is long-term, but functional recovery is realistic.

faqs.question: Is bipolar disorder curable?
faqs.answer: Bipolar disorder is not curable but is highly treatable. Mood stabilizers and atypical antipsychotics control acute episodes and prevent relapse; psychotherapy reduces relapse further; lifestyle stability protects against triggers. Most well-treated patients have long well intervals with few or no episodes for years at a time.

faqs.question: Will I need to take medication for life?
faqs.answer: For most patients, yes. After a single severe manic episode and after two or more episodes of any polarity, long-term maintenance treatment is recommended. Stopping mood stabilizers, particularly lithium, abruptly is associated with relapse in roughly 50% within 6 months and a rebound in suicide risk. Any change in treatment should be gradual and supervised by your psychiatrist.

faqs.question: What is rapid cycling bipolar disorder?
faqs.answer: Rapid cycling is defined as four or more mood episodes meeting full criteria within 12 months, separated by partial or full remission or by a polarity switch. It is more common in women, in bipolar II, and in patients exposed to antidepressant monotherapy or untreated hypothyroidism. Lithium and valproate are first-line; antidepressants are typically tapered off.

faqs.question: Can bipolar disorder appear after pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Yes. The postpartum period carries a 23-fold increase in risk of a manic or psychotic episode in women with a history of mood disorders. Postpartum psychosis is a psychiatric emergency requiring immediate care. Prophylactic mood stabilization during pregnancy and the early postpartum period is recommended for those with prior episodes.

faqs.question: Can children or teenagers have bipolar disorder?
faqs.answer: Yes, though the diagnosis is more difficult in children because of overlap with ADHD, disruptive mood dysregulation disorder, and developmental mood variability. About 75% of cases begin before age 30, and onset in mid-to-late adolescence is common. Pediatric bipolar disorder should be assessed by a child and adolescent psychiatrist.

faqs.question: What is the suicide risk in bipolar disorder?
faqs.answer: Lifetime suicide death rate is 10-15% and lifetime attempt rate is 30-40%, the highest of any psychiatric illness. Mixed states, depressive episodes, comorbid substance use, and recent hospital discharge are highest-risk periods. Lithium and structured psychotherapy reduce suicide risk substantially. If you are thinking of suicide, call or text 988 in the US.

faqs.question: Can cannabis cause bipolar disorder?
faqs.answer: Cannabis does not cause bipolar disorder in someone without genetic risk, but it can precipitate first manic episodes in vulnerable people and worsens long-term course in established disease. High-potency products, daily use, and onset in adolescence carry the greatest risk. Most psychiatrists recommend complete avoidance.

faqs.question: How is bipolar depression treated differently from regular depression?
faqs.answer: Bipolar depression is treated with mood stabilizers and atypical antipsychotics rather than antidepressant monotherapy. Quetiapine, lurasidone, cariprazine, lithium, lamotrigine, and the olanzapine-fluoxetine combination have the strongest evidence. Antidepressants are added cautiously and only with mood-stabilizer cover because of switch and rapid-cycling risk.

faqs.question: Is ECT used for bipolar disorder?
faqs.answer: Yes. Electroconvulsive therapy is the most effective acute treatment for severe, psychotic, catatonic, mixed, or treatment-resistant bipolar episodes, with response rates of 60-80%. ECT is safe in pregnancy and the elderly and works for both mania and depression. It remains underused given how effective it is.

faqs.question: What labs are needed for lithium treatment?
faqs.answer: Before starting lithium: serum creatinine and eGFR, electrolytes, calcium, TSH, complete blood count, pregnancy test in women of childbearing potential, and an ECG after age 40 or with cardiac history. During maintenance: lithium level, renal function, and TSH every 6-12 months. Stay well-hydrated and avoid sudden changes in sodium intake.

faqs.question: When should I go to the emergency department?
faqs.answer: Go immediately for active suicidal ideation with a plan or intent, psychotic mania with severe agitation or threats, sleep deprivation of 48+ hours during mania, mixed episodes with intense suicidality, postpartum mania or psychosis, and catatonia. In the US, calling or texting 988 connects you to crisis counselors who can also arrange emergency care.

metaTitle: Bipolar Disorder: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Bipolar disorder cycles between mania or hypomania and depression. Symptoms, diagnosis, lithium and modern treatments reviewed by our medical board.
